aliases: Special Beef Pho (Phở Bò Đặc Biệt)

english: Special Beef Pho (Phở Bò Đặc Biệt)

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Vietnamese pho; A0FHT (beef noodle soup) is the closest defensible analogue, representing a broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pho or Vietnamese beef noodle soup as of 2024.

main_ingredients: beef (various cuts: brisket, tendon, tripe, rare sliced beef)
fresh herbs (Thai basil, cilantro, green onion)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of special beef pho is approximately 500 g (one bowl), containing rice noodles, multiple cuts of beef, and aromatic bone broth with fresh herb garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Pho, beef (Phở bò) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for beef pho (Phở bò đặc biệt), confidence 0.95 as recorded in input. All per_100g values preserved from HPB canonical record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles as analogous food for pho noodle base
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for Vietnamese beef pho in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 61 is estimated from rice noodles (bánh phở), the dominant carbohydrate component of pho, which have a published GI of approximately 61 (medium range, 56–69) per Atkinson et al. 2008 and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 50.4) / 100 ≈ 30.7, based on 500 g serving with 10.08 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 290 mg/100 g from bone broth and fish sauce seasoning; a 500 g bowl delivers approximately 1450 mg sodium, representing 63% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit broth consumption, avoid adding hoisin sauce or sriracha at the table, and request reduced fish sauce in seasoning, as the liquid component carries the majority of sodium in this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles have a medium-to-high GI (~61), and a standard 500 g serving delivers approximately 50 g carbohydrate with only 4 g dietary fibre, producing a glycemic load of ~30.7 which is high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request a reduced noodle portion, increase the proportion of beef and bean sprouts to moderate the glycemic response, and avoid adding hoisin sauce which contributes additional sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 35 mg/100 g and sodium at 290 mg/100 g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; saturated fat data is not available from the HPB source but beef-based broth and multiple beef cuts contribute saturated fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, avoid fatty beef cuts (tendon, brisket), reduce broth intake to lower sodium and saturated fat exposure, and avoid condiments such as hoisin sauce and sriracha.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory cytokine production; chili (nightshade) may trigger individual RA flares.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting chicken pho (phở gà) to reduce arachidonic acid load, monitor individual tolerance to chili as a nightshade, and avoid hoisin sauce and sriracha.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Special Beef Pho (Phở Bò Đặc Biệt) is a Vietnamese rice noodle soup considered the premium or 'special' version of the classic phở bò, distinguished by the inclusion of multiple beef cuts in a single bowl — typically rare sliced beef (tái), well-done brisket (chín), beef tendon (gân), and tripe (sách). The dish is built on a deeply aromatic bone broth simmered for many hours with charred ginger, charred onion, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and cardamom, which gives the broth its characteristic golden colour and complex spiced-savoury flavour. The noodles used are flat dried rice noodles (bánh phở), which are soft and silky when cooked. The bowl is served with a garnish plate of fresh Thai basil, bean sprouts, lime wedges, and sliced chili, allowing diners to customise flavour and texture. Table condiments typically include hoisin sauce and sriracha. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 90 kcal, 5.48 g protein, 2.99 g fat, and 10.08 g carbohydrate with 0.8 g dietary fibre and 290 mg sodium. A standard 500 g bowl delivers approximately 450 kcal and 1450 mg sodium. The high sodium content from bone broth and fish sauce is the primary clinical concern for hypertension patients. The medium-to-high GI rice noodle base and high glycemic load (~30.7 per serving) make portion control important for type 2 diabetes management. The dish is culturally significant in Vietnam and is widely consumed in Singapore as a popular lunch and dinner option.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of pho?
What is phở bò đặc biệt?
What cuts of beef are in special beef pho?
Can I eat pho with high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of pho?
Is pho suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the canonical input record. GI is estimated from rice noodles as the analogous carbohydrate source using the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, which is the closest approved GI source available. Saturated fat and detailed fatty acid breakdown are not reported in the HPB source and are set to -1.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Phở Bò Đặc Biệt as of 2026. No supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA) was used to fill these gaps as the HPB canonical record is the primary source for this Singapore-context dish. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles (bánh phở) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for Vietnamese beef pho exists in the approved GI database. FoodEx2 code A0FHT (beef noodle soup) is used as the closest defensible analogue; no dedicated FoodEx2 code for Vietnamese pho exists as of 2024. Halal status is not confirmed — traditional pho uses fish sauce and beef; halal certification depends on the specific restaurant or preparation. The dish is not vegetarian or vegan due to beef and fish sauce content.
